data.title: Noem: Pretti shooting statement used 'best information we had at the time'
data.description:
Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said Thursday she was using the “best information we had at the time” when she initially responded to the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti over the weekend, the second U.S. citizen killed by federal law enforcement officers in Minneapolis this month.

Noem described a “very chaotic” situation on the ground in the aftermath of the shooting and told Fox News host Sean Hannity that she was “being relayed information from [agents and officers] on the ground.”

“We were using the best information we had at the time, seeking to be transparent with the American people, and get them what we knew to be true on the ground,” Noem responded, after Hannity asked if some of her initial statements were premature.

Noem is facing growing criticism over her rhetoric surrounding the shooting, with lawmakers on both sides of the aisle chiding her for rushing to compare Pretti, a 37-year-old ICU nurse, to a “domestic terrorist” immediately after he was shot and killed by a Border Patrol agent.

She claimed that Petti brandished a gun at officers and wanted to “inflict maximum damage on individuals and to kill law enforcement.”

“Violence against a government because of ideological reasons and for reasons to resist and to perpetuate violence. That is the definition of domestic terrorism,” Noem said on Saturday.

That narrative has been disputed by video footage, which showed Pretti holding a cellphone in his hand before he was pepper-sprayed and pushed to the ground. In the ensuing scuffle, agents can be seen confiscating Pretti’s firearm, which he was legally permitted to carry, from his waistband moments before shots are fired.

The agents involved have been put on administrative leave, a Department of Homeland Security (DHS) official told the Hill’s sister network, NewsNation.

Noem dodged questions from Hannity about why she used the term “domestic terrorism” to describe Pretti’s actions and those of 37-year-old Renee Good, who was shot and killed in her SUV by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer just weeks earlier.

“Why do you believe that’s appropriate? Because you used it in both cases,” Hannity asked.

“Well, Sean, we’re continuing to gather information, and the FBI leading this investigation is important to make sure that we talk about both these situations appropriately,” Noem replied.

Republicans and Democrats have been pushing for a deeper investigation into the Trump administration’s immigration enforcement tactics in Minnesota. Some have also called for Noem to resign or be impeached.

“What she’s done in Minnesota should be disqualifying. She should be out of a job,” Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) said on Monday. “It’s just amateurish. It’s terrible. It’s making the president look bad on policy that he won on.”

“She’s got to go — we are dead serious,” House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) told reporters in the Capitol on Thursday. “She needs to be put on ice permanently.” 

Noem pledged to “follow the investigation that the FBI is leading,” and dismissed her critics as “radicals” attacking her for “just doing my job.”  

“I’m following the law, enforcing the laws like President Trump promised that he would do to keep people safe in this country,” Noem said.

data.title: Take the battle against ICE off the streets and into the courts
data.description:
There is only one person to blame for the violence in Minneapolis that culminated in Renee Good and Alex Pretti being shot to death: Justice Clarence Thomas. 

Thomas authored the majority opinion in Egbert v. Boule, a case that will take its place in the hall of infamy right next to Dred Scott and Korematsu, which authorized rounding up Japanese-Americans and putting them in camps. In Egbert, the Supreme Court held that no one can bring constitutional claims for excessive force — or any constitutional claim — against federal officers enforcing immigration laws.

As a consequence, ICE and Border Patrol agents are effectively a law unto themselves.

If state law enforcement officers beat you up or break into your house, they can be sued under 42 USC 1983 for violating your constitutional rights. But under Egbert, if Border Patrol shoots you to death, your remedy is to file a grievance with the agency — a process in which you are unable to participate and may not appeal.

In short, under the current system, Kristi Noem and Greg Bovino are responsible for determining whether Pretti’s constitutional rights were violated when he was shot by Border Patrol agents. Even if you trusted either one of them as far as you could throw them, that’s not even a parody of due process, let alone justice.

The shootings of Good and Pretti are just the tip of the iceberg. Since July, the Department of Homeland Security has shot at at least 16 people. There are countless instances where ICE and the Border Patrol have made use of tear gas, pepper spray or physical assault. Pretti himself suffered a broken rib at the hands of ICE agents just a week before he was shot to death. Under current law, no one is accountable for any of this.  

But it doesn’t have to be this way. The Bivens Act would add just five words to 42 U.S. Code Section 1983 and make the federal government play by the same rules that states have lived under since 1871. 

Section 1983 is about more than getting damages from the government. In fact, that’s almost the least of it — Section 1983 is really about accountability. Armed with Section 1983, any citizen who has had their constitutional rights violated by an officer of the state can make that officer justify their actions to a court.

This kind of accountability is kryptonite for nascent tyranny. When law enforcement officers understand they may someday have to explain their actions, they think twice before engaging in bad behavior. ICE’s current anonymity, however, breeds contempt. Avoiding accountability is why Border Patrol agents and ICE officers wear masks. We still do not know the names of anyone involved in Pretti’s shooting by the Border Patrol. The only reason we know the name of Good’s shooter is that Noem accidentally outed him.

The fix is simple. but enacting it is not. The very last thing Noem and President Trump want is to have to justify such casual violence in court. Fortunately, Republicans need help to get the Department of Homeland Security funding bill through the Senate, so that congressional Democrats have some leverage to get this done.

Democrats are already demanding that several tweaks to the way Homeland Security operates be incorporated into the funding bill, but these are all small-bore reforms, like making agents remove their masks and wear body cameras. Forcing Republicans to pass the Bivens Act would be real institutional reform that would shift the balance of power away from the federal government and toward the citizens it is supposed to serve.

The Bivens Act would be good policy even under normal circumstances. These are not normal circumstances. Thousands of anonymous, armed agents have descended on an American city and have brought death and destruction in their wake, and all at the behest of a president who is supposed to be the ultimate guarantor of our liberties.

The Constitution does not enforce itself. Noem will not do it, and Congress cannot. So give us the tools and we will do the enforcement ourselves. The good citizens of Minneapolis have confronted tyranny in the streets. Now let them do so in the courts.

Chris Truax is an appellate attorney and charter member of the Society for the Rule of Law.

data.title: US has new favorite grocery store, data shows: Trader Joe's
data.description:
TAMPA, Fla. (WFLA) — Move over Publix, there’s a new favorite grocery store in town.

The latest data from the American Customer Satisfaction Index (ACSI) shows the Florida-based retailer, once known for iconic “hurricane cakes,” no longer sits at the top of the list for the top grocer in the U.S.

The ACSI takes into account several aspects of customers’ shopping experiences. Some of these include the speed of the checkout process, the convenience of store hours and location, the quality and freshness of meat and produce, and the general layout and cleanliness of the stores.

The companies — 19 were considered this year — are then ranked based on their scores on those “satisfaction benchmarks.”

In 2025, Publix was at the top of the list, tying with Trader Joe’s. This year, the latter’s fanbase only appears to be growing as the Hawaiian-themed chain earned a score of 86 out of 100 — a 2 percent increase from last year’s rating and just enough to push it past Publix.

Publix’s score of 84 out of 100 remained unchanged compared to 2025.

Regionally, however, Trader Joe’s was the leader in only one area: the West. This isn’t necessarily a surprise, considering California alone has more than 100 stores while the company is harder to come by in other regions.

In the Northeast, Aldi, ShopRite, and Walmart had the same score. Aldi came out on top in the Midwest and secured a narrow second – tied with H-E-B and Publix – in the South behind Sam’s Club.

Giant Eagle, a supermarket chain with stores in Pennsylvania, Ohio, West Virginia, Maryland and Indiana, ranked last on the ACSI list, dropping another 1 percent from last year with a score of 73 out of 100. Other low-scorers included Hy-Vee, Walmart and Albertsons.

Coming in third overall was Texas-based H-E-B, which earned a score of 83 out of 100, up from 82 last year. A recent report from customer data science company Dunnhumby put H-E-B at the top of its annual Retailer Preference Index for the fourth year in a row. Trader Joe’s came in at No. 7, while Publix did not appear in the top 10.

data.title: Trump addresses not nominating Hassett for Fed chair
data.description:
President Trump addressed his decision not to nominate National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett to chair the Federal Reserve, saying he did not want to move him from his current role. 

“[There] was great speculation that highly respected Kevin Hassett was going to be named Chairman of the Fed, and a great Chairman he would have been but, quite honestly, he is doing such an outstanding job working with me and my team at the White House, that I just didn’t want to let him go,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

“Kevin is indescribably good so, as the expression goes, ‘if you can’t do better, don’t try to fix it!’ Thank you Kevin for doing such a great job!” he added. 

In another post, the president revealed that he interviewed Federal Reserve Board member Christopher Waller, Black Rock executive Rick Rieder and others for the position. 

“They all would have been outstanding, and have a great and unlimited future with ‘TRUMP.’ Such amazing talent in our Country,” he wrote.

On Friday, Trump announced he picked former Federal Reserve board member Kevin Warsh to succeed Jerome Powell, whose term ends in May. Warsh was runner-up to Powell, who has received a lot of scrutiny from the administration in recent months over the central bank’s interest rate decisions and renovation plans, for the role during the president’s first term.

He is a former Morgan Stanley banker and served as economic adviser to former President George W. Bush. He, along with Hassett, had long been considered one of the frontrunners for the president’s nomination.

Warsh served on the Fed board from 2006 through 2011, where he became the youngest governor in the bank’s history and served as its liaison to Wall Street during the 2007-08 financial crisis.

data.title: DHS is out of control: Reining it in is worth a partial shutdown
data.description:
Government shutdowns are bad. Allowing heavily armed, out of control and poorly trained federal immigration agents to terrorize communities — abducting and imprisoning adults and children, breaking into homes and beating and killing nonviolent protesters — is worse. 

To stop the illegal and unconstitutional abuses of power by Immigration and Customs Enforcement, the Border Patrol and Customs and Border Protection — all part of the Department of Homeland Security — Senate Democrats, joined by some Republicans, justifiably blocked a vote to fund the department unless significant changes are made.

A temporary deal was later reached to fund the rest of government and extend Homeland Security funding for two weeks, but its fate was in doubt as of this writing, thanks to a Republican senator’s objection.

Either way, the Homeland Security funding bill is precisely the place for Democrats to take a principled stand.

Senate Democrats want to vote on funding for the Department of Homeland Security in a separate bill, rather than as part of a single measure funding all federal agencies. A separate bill would allow Democrats to force changes to the department to protect Americans and immigrants from abuses by ICE and other immigration agencies. That is a common-sense move that merits bipartisan support.

A partial government shutdown seemed unlikely before the deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti, two U.S. citizens tragically and wrongfully shot dead in Minneapolis by immigration officers.  

Before any serious investigation had been conducted of the killings, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and others in the Trump administration immediately and falsely called both Good and Pretti “domestic terrorists” and quickly claimed the two were trying to kill immigration enforcement officials. However, videos recorded by people at the scene of the deaths of the two protesters exposed those claims as lies.

Trump has sent thousands of ICE and Border Patrol agents and officers into cities run by Democrats — including more than 3,000 to the Minneapolis area. This sparked large protests, particularly in Minneapolis, by people viewing immigration forces as invaders arresting friends and neighbors who are productive members of their communities and who have done nothing illegal other than violating immigration laws.

Just about all Americans of every political persuasion agree that unauthorized immigrants who commit crimes in our country should be arrested, prosecuted and imprisoned or deported. But rather than carrying out his pledge to target only the “worst of the worst” criminals, Trump has ordered federal law enforcement officers to round up as many people as possible, including immigrants whose only crime was entering the U.S. illegally many years ago. American citizens mistakenly believed to be here illegally have also been arrested. 

Even five-year-old Liam Conejo Ramos was taken this month with his father by ICE agents in the Minneapolis area. The two were transported to an immigration detention center in Texas. The father’s lawyer said they had entered the U.S. legally and the father was applying for asylum.  

Mass arrests of unauthorized immigrants are not a wise use of the limited manpower and funding available for law enforcement. It’s just common sense for the Department of Homeland Security to adjust its priorities and end its most abusive and wasteful practices. 

As growing numbers of Americans — including some congressional Republicans — are concluding that Trump’s anti-immigrant campaign is going too far and harming too many people, Congress needs to exercise its oversight responsibility and take a close look at funding for the Department of Homeland Security. 

Rather than fight the efforts of Senate Democrats to do this, Senate Republicans should join with them to start doing their jobs and stop simply being a rubber stamp approving everything Trump demands. Doing this would both avert a partial government shutdown and improve immigration enforcement.  

Donna Brazile is a political strategist, a contributor to ABC News and former chair of the Democratic National Committee. She is the author of “Hacks: Inside the Break-ins and Breakdowns That Put Donald Trump in the White House.”

data.title: Don Lemon arrested by federal agents, attorney says
data.description:
Former CNN anchor Don Lemon was arrested by federal authorities in Los Angeles late Thursday night, according to his attorney.

Lemon, now an independent journalist and a frequent critic of President Trump and his allies, had drawn scrutiny from the administration in recent days over his coverage of anti-immigration enforcement protests at a church in Minnesota.

Developing

data.title: Live updates: Trump makes his Fed choice; Senate hits snag in reaching funding deal
data.description:
President Trump on Friday morning announced his choice to succeed Jerome Powell as chair of the Federal Reserve, naming Kevin Warsh to the role.

“I have known Kevin for a long period of time, and have no doubt that he will go down as one of the GREAT Fed Chairmen, maybe the best,” Trump said of Warsh, a former Fed governor who was runner-up to Powell for chair in 2017.

The government, meanwhile, is headed for a partial shutdown at midnight, as the Senate hit a snag in its deal-making. Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) placed a hold on the funding package, forcing leaders to punt a late Thursday vote. The chamber will reconvene at 11 a.m.

On the table is a package of five full-year funding bills and a stopgap measure funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) for two weeks. But Graham insists that the Senate not repeal a provision allowing senators to sue if their phone records were collected as part of former special counsel Jack Smith’s probe. The provision drew controversy after it was passed in a bill that ended the 43-day shutdown last year.

Trump has an executive order signing and a policy meeting on his Friday agenda.

Follow live here.

data.title: Trump on pulling back federal presence in Minnesota: 'Not at all'
data.description:
President Trump signaled Thursday that his administration would not pull back immigration enforcement efforts in Minnesota, contradicting comments made earlier in the day by border czar Tom Homan about a “draw down” of federal officials in the state.

“We keep our country safe. We’ll do whatever we can to keep our country safe,” Trump said ahead of the “Melania” film premiere at the Kennedy Center, responding to a reporter’s question about whether he would pull agents out of Minnesota.

“So, you’re not pulling back?” the reporter asked again.

“No, no. Not at all,” Trump replied.

The president’s remarks stand in stark contrast to Homan’s position outlined on Thursday, in which he stated that staff from Customs and Border Protection (CBP) and Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) were crafting a “draw down plan.”

“What does that look like based on the cooperation? What does that look like based on how many targets we have left to find?” the border czar said during a press conference in Minneapolis.

Homan, who was sent to replace U.S. Border Patrol commander-at-large Greg Bovino on the ground, said that Trump wanted to “fix” operations in the state following the fatal shootings of two U.S. citizens by federal immigration authorities.

The administration has made changes to “Operation Metro Surge” in the aftermath of the shooting, and Homan acknowledged that there may have been some missteps.

“I do not want to hear that everything that has been done here has been perfect. Nothing is ever perfect. Anything can be improved on,” Homan said Thursday. “What we’ve been working on is making this operation safer, more efficient, by the book.”

“The mission is going to improve because of changes we’re making internally,” he continued. “No agency organization is perfect. President Trump and I, along with others in the administration, have recognized that certain improvements could and should be made.”

Tensions have been rising over the administration’s immigration crackdown in Minnesota, where the recent shootings have sparked intense clashes between residents and federal officers, as well as widespread protests.

The Trump administration is now also facing stronger pushback from Republicans, and top officials from three government immigration agencies have been called to testify before the House and Senate Homeland Security committees next month.

A poll taken in the days before and after 37-year-old Alex Pretti was shot and killed — during what Department of Homeland Security (DHS) officials described as an “armed struggle,” despite video evidence to the contrary — found that more than half of Americans have “very little” confidence in ICE.

data.title: Gavin Newsom is ruining California — don't let him do it to the whole country
data.description:
How can anyone (other than maybe his immediate family) consider Gavin Newsom (D) a promising presidential candidate?  

Since Newsom became governor of California in 2019, he has presided over the ongoing decline of one of our country’s greatest states. California is rapidly losing population, losing businesses, and suffering repeated budget crises.

At a time when voters are concerned about affordability, California ranks as the second most expensive state in the U.S., thanks to high taxes, suffocating regulations and damaging progressive policies like the renewable energy mandates embraced by Newsom. It isn’t an accident that gasoline costs almost 50 percent more in California than the national average. 

Most recently, the threat of the nation’s first wealth tax being imposed on billionaires has the state’s most successful innovators departing in droves, taking their entrepreneurial genius and future endeavors with them.

Newsom has also shown himself to be a disastrous manager. California’s efforts to build a high-speed railroad have become a national joke. The estimated price tag for the hugely scaled-down project has skyrocketed from its original $33 billion 17 years ago to $135 billion now.

More recently, in the wake of Los Angeles’ disastrous wildfires (themselves reportedly at least partially the fault of Newsom’s poor forest oversight), rebuilding has been scant, despite bold promises from the governor. Some 16,000 homes were destroyed; so far there have only been 4,700 applications filed by those wanting to rebuild, and only 2,000 approved. That failure prompted President Trump to issue an executive order recently clearing the way for a rare federal takeover, promising to speed up permitting and construction.    

The final page in Newsom’s bleak record may turn out to be the discovery of massive fraud in his state. As in Minnesota, welfare programs in California appear to have been pilfered by thieves, all while Democrats in charge of the state looked the other way. A local federal prosecutor, calling Newsom the “king of fraud”, described one possible problem recently on a Fox News program. “California has spent $24 billion in the last five years on homelessness,” he pointed out. “And no one can account for where that money has really gone.”

Taxpayers don’t much like that; the exposure of fraud is what caused Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) to step back from running for a third term. Similar revelations could derail Newsom’s ambitions.

What exactly, other than an admittedly excellent head of hair, does Newsom bring to the table?

Certainly a boatload of Electoral College votes, but nearly any sentient Democrat can count on the deep-blue Golden State falling into his or her column. Maybe it’s the governor’s ability to shift with political winds. When the country seemed to oppose transgender athletes competing in women’s sports, for example, Newsom at least began to turn in that direction as well.  

Newsom’s competition for the 2028 Democratic nomination is not world-class. National polling shows Kamala Harris, a truly terrible campaigner, trailing just behind Newsom today. Others in the field include Pete Buttigieg, last seen whiffing on his responsibilities as Transportation secretary while on extended paternity leave during the COVID emergency, and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.), the Tik Tok contender who scares moderate Democrats to death. For what it’s worth, Polymarket has Newsom ahead by a mile, with AOC a distant second.

Since Newsom is leading, scrutiny of his accomplishments is warranted, including a review of his report card from the people who live in his state.

Last year, California led the U.S. in losing domestic migrants, with 229,077 leaving for greener pastures on net. From 2020 to 2024, California added more than 900,000 international migrants, but lost 1.46 million net domestic migrants, for a net loss of nearly a half million residents. Over the last 24 years, more than 4 million net domestic migrants have moved away.

People who study such things are surprised. In 2007, demographers estimated that, by 2050, California’s population would swell to 60 million. Today, the 2050 projection is for just 40 million. That’s what happens when you don’t treat your citizens well.  

The upshot of California’s population loss is that the state is poised to become less powerful politically. In the 2020 census, California lost one House seat; one group forecasts that, should current trends continue, the state will lose 4 more, dropping to 48 from the current 52.   

And if the Census were to stop including people in the country illegally — a change the Trump administration is trying to make — then California, home to more than 2 million undocumented people, would be much worse off. Democratic politicians have enticed illegal immigrants into their states by offering welfare benefits, access to drivers’ licenses and other perks, in part to maintain population numbers and thus political clout, even as residents flee for better-run states.

The vast number of illegal aliens living in California has put pressure on the state’s budget, since Newsom and his Democrat colleagues proudly provide free health care and education to all, regardless of citizenship status. As reported in the Los Angeles Times last March: “Gov. Gavin Newsom touted California’s role as the first state in the nation to offer healthcare to all income-eligible immigrants one year ago.” Unfortunately for Golden State residents, the cost of that healthcare, cited at $9.5 billion, was “already more than $3 billion above the budget estimate from last summer and is expected to grow.”

That’s not Newsom’s only budget problem. Notwithstanding being at the epicenter of a monster tech AI boom, California’s nonpartisan Legislative Analyst’s Office recently forecast the state will confront an $18 billion budget deficit this year, the fourth year in a row of yawning gaps. The numbers, according to that group, are only going to get worse.  

Newsom has made California smaller, poorer and less politically powerful. His many failings should be taken seriously; his presidential ambitions should not. 

Liz Peek is a former partner of major bracket Wall Street firm Wertheim and Company.
